findings: Tampa Bay top CRE deals in 2025 generated $1.46B across all asset classes
Tampa multifamily is the most transacted market in FL with $2.3B total sales volume trailing 12 months
631 retail transactions in Tampa over past 12 months at average 6.3% cap rate and $301/SF
20+ deals over $50M in 2024 (up from 13 in 2023), including four $100M+ trades
Water Street-specific comps limited in public data but institutional buyer activity is strong
The Stevedore residential building at Gasworx opening early 2026; Luisa and Olivette following in 2027
Gasworx signature office tower expected to complete later 2026 with 28,000 SF marketplace in early 2027
New entertainment district planned with 3,500-seat live music venue, hotel, retail, dining across from Benchmark International Arena
Channel District infrastructure improvements across 9 street segments in 15 phases, completion early 2027
Multifamily deliveries tapering sharply: ~7,400 units in 2025 down to ~3,500 in 2026 metro-wide
Tampa Land Development Code update expected to complete second half of 2026
Tampa private sector added 15,500 jobs YoY in June 2025, ranking #3 among FL metros
Lightcast ranks Tampa #8 among large metros nationally for talent attraction (prime-age, high-earning, college-educated workers)
Tampa Bay region projected to add 397K-547K new residents between 2025-2030
Tampa ranks #1 in net migration among comparable regions for young professionals and entrepreneurs
Tampa Bay EDC has attracted companies creating ~50,000 direct jobs over past 16 years across financial services, life sciences, healthcare, and advanced manufacturing
Class A multifamily averaging $2,305/unit (+1.4% YoY), Class B at $1,801 (+0.8%), Class C at $1,479 (+1.7%)
Tampa metro multifamily occupancy at 94.6% (August 2025), slightly below national 94.7%
Vacancy expected to close 2025 at 6.6%, down 60bps annually, ending three consecutive years of rising vacancy
Premium downtown office (4 & 5 Star) achieving $47+/SF; Water Street/Midtown West asking $60-70/SF
Downtown Tampa South - Islands submarket has lowest office vacancy at 2.92%
Supply tapering in 2026 expected to drive stronger rent gains
CBRE Lending Momentum Index rose 112% YoY in Q3 2025, highest since 2018
National CMBS issuance reached $115.2B through Nov 2025, highest since 2007
CMBS delinquency rate at 7.14% (Feb 2026), overall distress rate at 11.98%
Office CMBS delinquency over 12% in early 2026; $100B+ CMBS loans maturing in 2026 creating maturity wall
Tampa multifamily investment volume at $1.7B in 2025, second-highest in FL
Tampa positioned as supply-constrained market with stabilized multifamily opportunities
Prime rate at 6.75% as of Dec 2025; lenders tightening underwriting but activity increasing
Florida state preemption prohibits local rent control — landlord-friendly regulatory environment
Tampa has 23 designated Opportunity Zones; federal OZ benefits sunset after 2026 — urgency for OZ plays
Tampa multimodal impact fees updating for first time since 1989: from $955 to $5,284 for 2,000 SF home, phasing in 25% starting June 2026
Hillsborough County extended 0.5% infrastructure surtax from Nov 2026 to Dec 2041
FL legislature considering significant property tax relief (HJR 201) that could eliminate non-school property taxes on homestead
Tampa Future Land Use Update continued to Feb 2026 to incorporate SB 180 (anti-restrictive zoning legislation)

key_findings.finding: Water Street/Channel District is in late-stage development buildout with $3B master plan nearing 2027 completion — acquisition window for stabilized assets opening as initial phases deliver
Multifamily supply delivery dropping 53% from 7,400 units (2025) to 3,500 units (2026) metro-wide — supply relief should accelerate rent growth
Tampa ranks #1 in net migration for young professionals and #8 nationally for talent attraction — structural demand tailwind for mixed-use assets
Premium office at Water Street commanding $60-70/SF vs. broader Tampa at $47/SF — significant rent premium validates neighborhood positioning
Impact fee increase from $955 to $5,284 (phasing June 2026) will raise development costs and slow future supply — benefits existing asset holders
OZ benefits sunsetting after 2026 creates urgency for any remaining OZ-eligible acquisitions in Tampa's 23 designated zones
CMBS maturity wall of $100B+ in 2026 with office delinquency over 12% may create distressed acquisition opportunities, though Water Street office assets likely insulated by premium positioning
New entertainment district (3,500-seat venue, hotel, retail) will significantly enhance neighborhood amenity profile and drive foot traffic to adjacent assets

feature_gaps.gap: No automated access to Hillsborough County deed transfer and lis pendens data — had to rely on aggregated market reports instead of transaction-level data
Cannot access CoStar/RealPage for submarket-level rent comps specific to Water Street — all rent data is metro-wide
No automated building permit API for City of Tampa — could not quantify exact pipeline by address in the zone
Missing BLS QCEW drill-down by Census tract for employment density within the zone specifically
No CMBS loan-level data to identify specific Water Street/Channel District loans approaching maturity
Ability to overlay OZ boundary maps with zone geometry to identify OZ-eligible parcels would improve regulatory scoring
No cap rate trend chart for the submarket over time — would help visualize pricing trajectory
Score delta tracking across rotation runs not yet implemented — no previous brief baseline for comparison
